part.text:
# Manage Git Remotes with Jujutsu

## Background
You are starting a new project and need to set up your version control using `jj` (Jujutsu). You will initialize the repository and configure multiple Git remotes to collaborate with others.

## Requirements
- Initialize a new `jj` repository colocated with Git in the project directory.
- Add a Git remote named `origin` pointing to `https://github.com/example/repo.git`.
- Add a Git remote named `upstream` pointing to `https://github.com/example/upstream.git`.
- Rename the `upstream` remote to `public`.
- Remove the `origin` remote.

## Implementation
1. Navigate to the project path.
2. Initialize a colocated `jj` repository.
3. Use `jj git remote add` to add the `origin` and `upstream` remotes.
4. Use `jj git remote rename` to rename `upstream` to `public`.
5. Use `jj git remote remove` to remove the `origin` remote.

## Constraints
- **Project path**: `/home/user/myproject`
- You must use `jj` commands to manage the remotes.

# Manage Git Remotes with Jujutsu

## Background
You are starting a new project and need to set up your version control using `jj` (Jujutsu). You will initialize the repository and configure multiple Git remotes to collaborate with others.

## Requirements
- Initialize a new `jj` repository colocated with Git in the project directory.
- Add a Git remote named `origin` pointing to `https://github.com/example/repo.git`.
- Add a Git remote named `upstream` pointing to `https://github.com/example/upstream.git`.
- Rename the `upstream` remote to `public`.
- Remove the `origin` remote.

## Implementation
1. Navigate to the project path.
2. Initialize a colocated `jj` repository.
3. Use `jj git remote add` to add the `origin` and `upstream` remotes.
4. Use `jj git remote rename` to rename `upstream` to `public`.
5. Use `jj git remote remove` to remove the `origin` remote.

output.output:
Initialized repo in "."
Hint: Running `git clean -xdf` will remove `.jj/`!

input.command: cd /home/user/myproject && jj git remote add origin https://github.com/example/repo.git && jj git remote add upstream https://github.com/example/upstream.git && jj git remote rename upstream public && jj git remote remove origin

cd /home/user/myproject && jj git remote add origin https://github.com/example/repo.git && jj git remote add upstream https://github.com/example/upstream.git && jj git remote rename upstream public && jj git remote remove origin

cd /home/user/myproject && jj git remote add origin https://github.com/example/repo.git && jj git remote add upstream https://github.com/example/upstream.git && jj git remote rename upstream public && jj git remote remove origin

input.result:
I have successfully initialized the Jujutsu repository and configured the remotes as requested.

Here are the actions taken:
1. Created the directory `/home/user/myproject` and navigated to it.
2. Initialized a colocated Jujutsu repository using `jj git init --colocate`.
3. Added the `origin` remote pointing to `https://github.com/example/repo.git`.
4. Added the `upstream` remote pointing to `https://github.com/example/upstream.git`.
5. Renamed the `upstream` remote to `public`.
6. Removed the `origin` remote.

The final state of the remotes correctly shows only `public` pointing to `https://github.com/example/upstream.git`.

I have successfully initialized the Jujutsu repository and configured the remotes as requested.

Here are the actions taken:
1. Created the directory `/home/user/myproject` and navigated to it.
2. Initialized a colocated Jujutsu repository using `jj git init --colocate`.
3. Added the `origin` remote pointing to `https://github.com/example/repo.git`.
4. Added the `upstream` remote pointing to `https://github.com/example/upstream.git`.
5. Renamed the `upstream` remote to `public`.
6. Removed the `origin` remote.

The final state of the remotes correctly shows only `public` pointing to `https://github.com/example/upstream.git`.
